---
title: "Common Persimmon (Diospyros virginiana)"
slug: "diospyros-virginiana"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/474812377/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Common Persimmon"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Magnoliopsida"
tax_order: "Ericales"
tax_family: "Ebenaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Magnoliopsida", "Ericales", "Ebenaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/474812377/medium.jpg" alt="Common Persimmon" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/hoskissbirder">
hoskissbirder</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/264374869">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Magnoliopsida
-   **Order:** Ericales
-   **Family:** Ebenaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=83435)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Common species in Florida found in forests, old fields, and roadsides.

## Natural history notes

A deciduous tree characterized by its blocky, dark bark, oval leaves, and orange fruits that ripen in fall. In Florida, it is found in a variety of habitats including forests, old fields, and roadsides. It flowers in spring, and the fruits become sweet when fully ripe, providing an important food source for wildlife.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/83435-Diospyros-virginiana)
-   [Local profile](https://florida.plantatlas.usf.edu/plant/species/3858)
